name: Present Tense
summary: Conjugate regular verbs in the present tense, the workhorse tense of everyday German.
explanation:
To conjugate a regular German verb, take the stem (the infinitive minus -en) and add an ending that matches the subject. The infinitive 'spielen' (to play) has the stem 'spiel-'.

German present tense covers both English 'I play' and 'I am playing'. There is no separate continuous form, so 'ich spiele' means both.

When a stem ends in -t or -d (like 'arbeiten', to work), an extra -e is inserted before the du, er/sie/es, and ihr endings to make it pronounceable: du arbeitest, er arbeitet.
pattern: stem + e / st / t / en / t / en
commonMistakes: Forgetting the -st ending for 'du': it is 'du spielst', not 'du spielt'.
Using an English continuous form. German has no 'ich bin spielen'; just say 'ich spiele'.
Skipping the inserted -e with verbs like arbeiten: it is 'er arbeitet', not 'er arbeitt'.
Mixing up 'wir/sie/Sie' (all take -en) with the singular forms.
Capitalising the formal 'Sie' but not the informal 'sie' (they). Both are spelled s-i-e but Sie is always capitalised.
tip: The wir, sie (they), and Sie (formal you) forms are always identical to the infinitive. Learn those three for free.
